answer_short: Electricity is the movement or buildup of electric charge, usually involving electrons.
answer_extra: In circuits, electricity usually means charges moving through a complete path. Static electricity means charge is built up in one place.
related_terms: electric current; static electricity; electric charge

answer_short: Matter is anything that has mass and takes up space.
answer_extra: Atoms are the tiny particles that make up matter. Understanding atoms helps explain electric charge.

answer_short: An atom is a tiny particle of matter made of protons, neutrons, and electrons.
answer_extra: Protons and neutrons are in the nucleus. Electrons move around the nucleus.

answer_short: A proton is a positively charged particle found in the nucleus of an atom.
answer_extra: The number of protons helps identify the element.

answer_short: A neutron is a neutral particle found in the nucleus of an atom.
answer_extra: Neutral means it has no electric charge.

answer_short: An electron is a negatively charged particle found outside the nucleus of an atom.
answer_extra: Electric charge is often transferred when electrons move from one object or material to another.
related_terms: atom; current; static electricity; negative charge

answer_short: Electric charge is a property of matter that can be positive or negative.
answer_extra: Objects can become charged when they gain or lose electrons. Like charges repel, and opposite charges attract.
related_terms: positive charge; negative charge; static electricity; electric field
source_ref: PDF p.1, p.6, p.11; worksheet photos

answer_short: A positive charge means there are more protons than electrons, or fewer electrons than needed for neutrality.
answer_extra: Protons are positive. In many static electricity problems, an object becomes positive by losing electrons.

answer_short: A negative charge means there are more electrons than protons.
answer_extra: Electrons are negative. An object often becomes negative by gaining electrons.

answer_short: Neutral means an object has no overall electric charge.
answer_extra: In a neutral atom, the number of protons equals the number of electrons.

answer_short: Static electricity is a buildup of electric charge on an object.
answer_extra: It often happens when electrons transfer by friction, such as rubbing a balloon on hair or cloth.
source_ref: PDF p.1, p.2, p.6, p.11; worksheet photos

answer_short: An electric field is the area around a charged object where it can push or pull other charges.
answer_extra: An electric field lets charged objects exert forces at a distance.
related_terms: electric charge; force at a distance; induction

answer_short: An electroscope is a device used to detect electric charge.
answer_extra: It can show that charge is present, often by the movement or spreading of lightweight parts.

answer_short: A conductor is a material that allows electric charge or current to move through it easily.
answer_extra: Metals are usually good conductors because their electrons can move more freely.
source_ref: PDF p.1, p.4, p.6, p.11; worksheet photos

answer_short: An insulator is a material that does not allow electric charge or current to move through it easily.
answer_extra: Rubber, plastic, glass, and dry wood are common examples of insulators.
source_ref: PDF p.1, p.4, p.6, p.11; worksheet photos

answer_short: Static discharge is the sudden movement of built-up electric charge from one place to another.
answer_extra: A small shock after walking on carpet is static discharge. Lightning is a large static discharge.

answer_short: Grounding is giving electric charge a safe path to flow into Earth or another large conductor.
answer_extra: Grounding can remove excess charge and helps protect buildings and people from dangerous discharge.

aliases: charging by conduction; direct contact transfer
answer_short: Conduction is transfer of charge by direct contact.
answer_extra: For example, a charged object can transfer charge by touching another object.

answer_short: Friction can transfer charge when two objects rub together.
answer_extra: Rubbing a balloon on hair or rubbing socks on carpet can transfer electrons and build up static electricity.

aliases: charging by induction; charge from a distance
answer_short: Induction is when a charged object causes charges in another object to shift without touching it.
answer_extra: A charged balloon picking up small paper pieces or bending a thin stream of water are examples of induction.

aliases: current; flow of charge; flow of electrons; I
answer_short: Electric current is the flow of electric charge through a material or circuit.
answer_extra: Current is measured in amperes, or amps. In many circuit problems, I = V / R.
source_ref: PDF p.1, p.7, p.8, p.11; worksheet photos

aliases: amp; amps; A; unit for current
answer_short: An ampere, or amp, is the unit used to measure electric current.
answer_extra: If a problem asks for current, the answer usually has units of amps or A.

aliases: voltage; potential difference; electric potential difference; V; volts
answer_short: Voltage difference is the push that causes electric charges to flow in a circuit.
answer_extra: Voltage difference is measured in volts. If resistance stays the same, more voltage produces more current.
source_ref: PDF p.1, p.7, p.8, p.11; worksheet photos

aliases: volts; V; unit for voltage; unit for potential difference
answer_short: A volt is the unit used to measure voltage difference.
answer_extra: If a problem asks for voltage or potential difference, the answer usually has units of volts or V.

answer_short: Resistance is opposition to the flow of electric current.
answer_extra: Resistance is measured in ohms. If voltage stays the same, more resistance means less current.
source_ref: PDF p.1, p.8, p.11; worksheet photos

answer_short: An ohm is the unit used to measure resistance.
answer_extra: If a problem asks for resistance, the answer usually has units of ohms or Ω.

aliases: Ohm's Law; ohms law; VIR; V = IR; I = V/R; R = V/I
answer_short: Ohm’s Law relates voltage, current, and resistance.
answer_extra: The three common forms are I = V / R, V = I × R, and R = V / I.
source_ref: PDF p.1, p.8, p.10, p.11; worksheet photos

answer_short: A circuit is a path that electric current can travel through.
answer_extra: A working circuit needs a power source, conducting path, and usually a load. Current flows only when the path is complete.
related_terms: closed circuit; open circuit; battery; wire
source_ref: PDF p.1, p.7, p.12; worksheet photos

answer_short: A closed circuit has a complete path, so current can flow.
answer_extra: When a switch is closed, the circuit is connected and devices can turn on.
source_ref: PDF p.1, p.11, p.20; worksheet photos

answer_short: An open circuit has a break in the path, so current cannot flow.
answer_extra: When a switch is open, the circuit is disconnected and devices turn off.

answer_short: A battery is a power source that converts chemical energy into electrical energy.
answer_extra: A battery provides the voltage difference that pushes charge through a circuit. Batteries in series can add their voltages.
source_ref: PDF p.1, p.7, p.11; worksheet photos

answer_short: A wire is a conductor that connects parts of a circuit and gives current a path to flow.
answer_extra: Wires are often made from metals such as copper because metals conduct electricity well.

aliases: circuit switch; open switch; closed switch
answer_short: A switch is a device that opens or closes a circuit.
answer_extra: An open switch stops current. A closed switch allows current to flow.
source_ref: PDF p.1, p.13, p.14, p.20; worksheet photos

answer_short: A light bulb is a load that converts electrical energy into light and heat.
answer_extra: Bulb brightness depends on current and power. In simple circuits, more current usually means a brighter bulb.

answer_short: A resistor is a circuit part that adds resistance and limits current.
answer_extra: In circuit diagrams, a resistor is often shown as a zigzag line or a rectangle, depending on the notation.

answer_short: An ammeter is a meter used to measure electric current.
answer_extra: An ammeter should be placed in series so the current being measured flows through it.
source_ref: PDF p.1, p.14, p.17, p.22; worksheet photos

answer_short: A voltmeter is a meter used to measure voltage difference.
answer_extra: A voltmeter should be placed across, or parallel to, the part being measured.
source_ref: PDF p.1, p.13, p.15; worksheet photos

answer_short: A series circuit has only one path for current to flow.
answer_extra: If one part of a series circuit opens or breaks, current stops through the whole circuit. Total resistance in series is the sum of the resistances.
source_ref: PDF p.1, p.12-p.18, p.21; worksheet photos

answer_short: A parallel circuit has more than one path for current to flow.
answer_extra: If one branch opens, other branches may still work. Adding branches in parallel usually decreases total resistance.
source_ref: PDF p.1, p.12-p.18, p.21; worksheet photos

answer_short: A branch is one separate path in a parallel circuit.
answer_extra: Each branch can hold a device such as a bulb, resistor, or switch.

answer_short: A complex circuit combines series and parallel parts in the same circuit.
answer_extra: To analyze it, break it into smaller series and parallel sections.

answer_short: A fuse is a safety device that breaks a circuit if too much current flows.
answer_extra: A fuse helps prevent overheating by opening the circuit.

answer_short: A circuit breaker is a reusable safety switch that opens a circuit when too much current flows.
answer_extra: Circuit breakers help prevent overheating and electrical fires.

answer_short: Power is the rate at which a device converts electrical energy into another form of energy.
answer_extra: Electrical power is often measured in watts. A brighter bulb usually uses more power.

answer_short: Magnetism is a force related to magnets and moving electric charges.
answer_extra: Magnetic forces can attract or repel and can act at a distance through magnetic fields.

answer_short: A magnet is an object that has a magnetic field and can attract some materials.
answer_extra: Magnets have north and south poles. Opposite poles attract, and like poles repel.

answer_short: Magnetic force is the push or pull between magnetic objects or moving charges.
answer_extra: It is strongest near the poles of a magnet and gets weaker with distance.

answer_short: A magnetic field is the area around a magnet or moving charge where magnetic force can act.
answer_extra: Magnetic field lines form closed loops and are often shown from north to south outside a magnet.

answer_short: A magnetic pole is one end of a magnet where magnetic force is strongest.
answer_extra: Magnets always have both a north and a south pole; cutting a magnet makes smaller magnets with both poles.

answer_short: A magnetic domain is a tiny region inside a material where magnetic fields line up.
answer_extra: In magnetic materials, many domains are aligned. In nonmagnetic materials, domains point in different directions.

answer_short: Ferromagnetism is strong magnetism found in materials whose domains can line up.
answer_extra: Iron, nickel, and cobalt are common ferromagnetic materials.

answer_short: An electromagnet is a magnet made by electric current flowing through a coil of wire, often around metal.
answer_extra: You can make an electromagnet stronger by adding more wire loops or increasing current.

answer_short: An electric motor uses electricity and magnetism to turn electrical energy into motion.
answer_extra: Motors use magnetic forces to make parts spin.

answer_short: A generator uses motion and magnetism to produce electrical energy.
answer_extra: A generator is related to a motor, but it converts motion into electricity instead of electricity into motion.
